schools.summary: Paul, writing to a beloved and trusting church, does not assert his apostleship but joins Timothy with himself simply as 'servants (bondservants) of Jesus Christ' — a title of affectionate humility; 'bishop (episkopos, overseer)' and 'presbyter (elder)' name the same office in the New Testament, used interchangeably (Acts 20:17, 28; Titus 1:5-7), so the 'bishops' here are the plural body of pastors of the one church, not prelates of a superior order. Barnes argues at length against the prelatical reading — there were but two orders (bishops/elders and deacons), and a plurality of 'bishops' in one city refutes diocesan episcopacy. Ellicott grants the identity but holds the later monarchical episcopate developed providentially in the early second century, under the sanction of St. John (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): the titles 'bishop' and 'presbyter' are synonymous, as Jerome also testifies; the custom of applying 'bishop' exclusively to one who presides over the presbyters crept in after the apostle's time and rests on no Scripture authority but on human custom — and from the corrupted use of the word came the evil of one usurping dominion over his colleagues.

debates.question: Are 'bishop' and 'presbyter' the same office in the New Testament, or distinct orders?

sides.position: The same office — bishop = presbyter/elder; only two NT orders (bishops/elders and deacons), the prelatical three-order reading rejected
Identical in the NT, but the monarchical episcopate developed later as a legitimate, providential order under St. John

themes: Paul and Timotheus, the servants of Jesus Christ
to all the saints in Christ Jesus which are at Philippi, with the bishops and deacons
the greeting to the saints, bishops, and deacons
bishop and presbyter the same office

schools.summary: Paul's customary greeting, nearly the same in all his epistles: 'grace (free favour) be unto you, and peace (its fruit)' — the twofold blessing, grace the source and peace the effect; coming jointly 'from God our Father' (the fountain of all blessing) and 'from the Lord Jesus Christ' (the channel through whom they reach us) — Father and Son named together; no peace without grace, for inward peace springs from a sense of God's favour (Barnes, Gill, Henry).

themes: Grace be unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ
the apostolic blessing of grace and peace
grace the source, peace the fruit
Father and Son the source

schools.summary: Paul opens his thanksgiving: 'I thank my God upon every remembrance (at all my recollection) of you' — every time he thinks of the Philippians, his heart turns to grateful thanks to God; the affectionate 'my God' claiming God as his own in covenant relation, his thanks flowing from a personal, intimate faith; the Philippians, who had been so good to him, remembered with unmixed joy (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: I thank my God upon every remembrance of you
Paul's thanksgiving at every remembrance
my God, the covenant relation
the Philippians remembered with joy

schools.summary: Paul prays 'always in every prayer of mine for you all' — his intercession for the Philippians is constant, embracing the whole church ('you all,' a phrase frequent in this epistle); 'making request with joy' — the distinctive note of this epistle, Paul's prayers for them not anxious or grieved (as for some churches) but full of gladness, for they gave him so much cause for rejoicing (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Wesley: the joy that marks Paul's prayers for the Philippians reflects the peculiar love and confidence between him and this church, the only one to which he writes without rebuke.

themes: always in every prayer of mine for you all making request with joy
Paul's joyful prayer for them all
constant intercession with gladness
the peculiar love for this church

schools.summary: The reason for Paul's joyful thanks: 'your fellowship (koinonia — partnership, sharing, participation) in the gospel' — the Philippians' active partnership in the cause of the gospel, including their support of Paul; 'from the first day (of their conversion) until now' — steadfast and unbroken, they had not, like some, grown cold but persevered in gospel partnership; the fellowship including both their sharing in the gospel's blessings and their active participation in its furtherance (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: For your fellowship in the gospel from the first day until now
their fellowship in the gospel
steadfast gospel partnership
persevering from the first day

schools.summary: Paul's assured confidence: 'he (God) which hath begun a good work in you' — the work of grace in the Philippians is God's own work, begun by him in their conversion and regeneration; God 'will perform (epitelesei — carry on, complete, perfect) it until the day of Jesus Christ' — God will not abandon what he began but will carry the work of grace through to completion at Christ's return; we must not trust outward appearances but the new creation God works (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Calvin: the believer's confidence is not in himself but in God, who, having begun the work freely, will faithfully finish it; what God begins he completes — the perseverance of the saints rests entirely on God's faithfulness, not on human steadfastness.

themes: he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ
God will complete the good work he began
the perseverance of the saints
God's faithfulness the ground

schools.summary: Paul's ground for his confidence: it is 'meet (right, fitting)' for him to think well of them, 'because I have you in my heart' — he carries the Philippians in his deepest affection; they had shared with him 'both in my bonds (his imprisonment) and in the defence and confirmation (the vindicating and establishing) of the gospel' — standing with him in his sufferings and his gospel work; 'ye all are partakers of my grace' — joint-sharers in the grace given to Paul, their fellow-suffering binding them to him (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Calvin: the Philippians were companions of Paul in the grace of God, sharing both his bonds and his gospel labour; fellow-sufferers in the cause of God should be dear one to another, and such partnership is itself a token of grace.

themes: because I have you in my heart
both in my bonds, and in the defence and confirmation of the gospel, ye all are partakers of my grace
Paul's love for the Philippians as partakers of his grace
fellow-sufferers in the gospel

schools.summary: Paul calls God to witness the depth of his love: 'God is my record (witness), how greatly I long after (yearn for, deeply desire) you all' — appealing to God himself for the sincerity and intensity of his affection; 'in the bowels (splanchna — the inward parts, the seat of tender affection) of Jesus Christ' — Paul loves them not merely with his own love but with the very tenderness of Christ, as though Christ's own heart of compassion beat in him (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Henry: Paul's affection for the Philippians flows from his union with Christ; he loves them with Christ's love, in Christ's heart, the tenderness of the Saviour himself working through the apostle.

themes: God is my record, how greatly I long after you all in the bowels of Jesus Christ
Paul's yearning love in the affection of Christ
loving them with Christ's tenderness
the apostle's Christ-filled love

schools.summary: Paul's prayer for the Philippians: 'that your love may abound (overflow, increase) yet more and more' — not that they begin to love, for they already do, but that their love grow ever greater; 'in knowledge (epignosis — full, accurate knowledge) and in all judgment (aisthesis — discernment, moral perception)' — love guided and informed by knowledge and discernment, not blind or undiscriminating, a wise and intelligent love (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: love is to be regulated by knowledge and discernment, that it may not err through misguided zeal; true love grows in proportion as it is enlightened, discerning what is right and good.

themes: that your love may abound yet more and more in knowledge and in all judgment
Paul's prayer that their love abound in knowledge
love guided by knowledge and discernment
a wise and intelligent love

schools.summary: The purpose of the abounding, knowing love: 'that ye may approve (dokimazein — test and approve, or distinguish) things that are excellent (ta diapheronta — the things that differ, the better things)' — that they may discern and choose what is truly best; 'that ye may be sincere (eilikrines — pure, unmixed, genuine, as tested by sunlight) and without offence (blameless, not causing others to stumble) till the day of Christ' — inwardly pure and outwardly blameless, maintained steadfastly until his return (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the discerning love issues in the choice of what is excellent, and so in a purity and blamelessness that endures to the day of Christ; holiness is to be steadfast, kept whole until the end.

themes: That ye may approve things that are excellent
that ye may be sincere and without offence till the day of Christ
approving the excellent and being sincere
holiness enduring till the day of Christ

schools.summary: The further mark: 'being filled with the fruits of righteousness' — the inward purity issues in a full harvest of righteous deeds, the holy life fruitful in every good work; 'which are by (through) Jesus Christ' — produced not by human effort alone but through Christ, the source of all true righteousness in the believer, the fruit growing from union with him; 'unto the glory and praise of God' — the whole aim being God's glory (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the fruits of righteousness come by Jesus Christ, not from ourselves; and their end is the glory and praise of God, that he may be honoured in the holiness his grace produces.

themes: Being filled with the fruits of righteousness, which are by Jesus Christ
unto the glory and praise of God
filled with the fruits of righteousness
holiness to God's glory through Christ

schools.summary: Paul turns to his own situation: 'the things which happened unto me' — his arrest, imprisonment, and trials at Rome, which the Philippians might have feared had hindered his work; but they 'have fallen out (turned out) rather unto the furtherance (prokope — the advance, progress) of the gospel' — God had overruled his imprisonment to advance the very cause it seemed to threaten (Barnes, Robertson).
Henry: God brings good out of evil; what seemed a setback to the gospel God turned into its furtherance, so that Paul's chains served Christ and the cause advanced through his sufferings.

themes: the things which happened unto me have fallen out rather unto the furtherance of the gospel
Paul's imprisonment furthered the gospel
God overruling his bonds for good
good brought out of evil

schools.summary: The first way his imprisonment furthered the gospel: 'my bonds in Christ (my chains, borne for Christ) are manifest (become known, openly recognized) in all the palace (praitorion — the praetorian guard, or the praetorian barracks)' — even among the imperial guard and the household of Caesar it became known that Paul was a prisoner for Christ, not for any crime, the gospel reaching the very heart of imperial power; 'and in all other places' his testimony spread widely (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: Paul's chains preached Christ even in the palace; that the reason for his bonds was known to be the gospel, not any crime, turned his imprisonment into a public testimony at the centre of the empire.

themes: my bonds in Christ are manifest in all the palace, and in all other places
Paul's bonds made known throughout the palace
the gospel reaching the imperial guard
his chains a testimony

schools.summary: The second way his imprisonment advanced the gospel: 'many of the brethren in the Lord, waxing confident (growing bold, gaining courage) by my bonds, are much more bold to speak the word without fear' — Paul's steadfast example in chains emboldened other believers; the paradox that his imprisonment, which might have frightened others into silence, instead gave them courage, seeing his constancy and God's support, so that they preached the gospel the more boldly (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: Paul's constancy under bonds, far from discouraging the brethren, animated them; the example of his fearless endurance multiplied the preachers of the word and emboldened them to speak without fear.

themes: many of the brethren in the Lord, waxing confident by my bonds, are much more bold to speak the word without fear
the brethren emboldened by Paul's bonds
chains giving courage rather than fear
the gospel multiplied

schools.summary: Paul distinguishes two groups now preaching: 'some indeed preach Christ even of envy and strife (from jealousy and rivalry, ill-disposed toward Paul); and some also of good will (from sincere affection and right motives)'; some preached Christ from envy of Paul — perhaps jealous of his influence, or seeking to build their own party — using even the preaching of the true gospel as a means of rivalry against him; the difference was not in the message (both preached Christ) but in the motive (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: Some indeed preach Christ even of envy and strife; and some also of good will
some preach Christ from envy, others from good will
the same gospel from opposite hearts
the difference of motive

schools.summary: The description of the ill-disposed group (following the better manuscript order): 'the one preach Christ of contention (from party-spirit, faction, rivalry), not sincerely (not with pure motives)' — their preaching a means of self-promotion and opposition; 'supposing to add affliction (trouble, distress) to my bonds' — they thought, by their successful preaching, to increase Paul's grief, perhaps by stirring envy or undermining his influence while he lay helpless in prison; their malice failed, for Paul rejoiced that Christ was preached (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: The one preach Christ of contention, not sincerely, supposing to add affliction to my bonds
the insincere preachers seeking to add to Paul's affliction
preaching from party-spirit
malice that failed

schools.summary: The description of the well-disposed group: 'the other (preach Christ) of love' — from sincere love, both to Christ and to Paul, their preaching flowing from right affection; 'knowing that I am set (appointed, placed) for the defence (apologia — the vindication) of the gospel' — they understood that Paul's imprisonment was for the gospel's defence, and their preaching supported, not undermined, his cause; over against the contentious preachers, these preach in sympathy with the imprisoned apostle (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: But the other of love, knowing that I am set for the defence of the gospel
the sincere preachers who preach from love
preaching in sympathy with Paul
the right motive

schools.summary: Paul brushes aside the question of motives with a magnanimous 'what then?' — what does it ultimately matter? — rising above any personal grievance; 'whether in pretence (with false, self-serving motives) or in truth (sincerely), Christ is preached' — the great fact, that Christ is proclaimed, outweighs every consideration of the preachers' motives; 'and I therein do rejoice, yea, and will rejoice' (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

Calvin: Paul rejoices, and resolves to keep rejoicing, simply because Christ is made known — forgetting his own injury in his joy for the gospel's spread; his concern is Christ's glory, not his own vindication.

themes: whether in pretence, or in truth, Christ is preached; and I therein do rejoice, yea, and will rejoice
Paul's joy that Christ is preached whatever the motive
the great fact outweighing motives
selfless joy in the gospel's spread

schools.summary: Paul's settled confidence: 'I know that this (his whole situation — imprisonment, the preaching, all of it) shall turn to my salvation' — either his deliverance from prison, or (more deeply) his final salvation, all things working for his good; 'through your prayer' — Paul ascribes much to the intercession of the Philippians, valuing and relying on their prayers; 'and the supply (epichoregia — the abundant provision, support) of the Spirit of Jesus Christ' — the Holy Spirit's supply of grace and strength upholding him (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): the Spirit is called 'the Spirit of Jesus Christ' (a witness to the bond of the Son and the Spirit); by his abundant supply of grace Paul is upheld, so that even his bonds shall turn to his salvation.

themes: I know that this shall turn to my salvation through your prayer
the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ
Paul's confidence that all will turn to his salvation
upheld by prayer and the Spirit

schools.summary: Paul's 'earnest expectation (apokaradokia — eager, intense longing, the head stretched forward) and hope' that 'in nothing I shall be ashamed' — that he will not be put to shame, his faith and courage not failing under trial; 'but that with all boldness (freedom, courage), as always, so now also Christ shall be magnified (exalted, made great) in my body' — that Christ may be honoured and glorified in and through Paul, in his very person, 'whether it be by life, or by death' (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: Paul cares not whether Christ is magnified by his life (if released) or by his death (if martyred) — he desires only that Christ be glorified, by whichever outcome; self is wholly swallowed up in zeal for Christ's honour.

themes: that in nothing I shall be ashamed, but... Christ shall be magnified in my body
whether it be by life, or by death
Christ magnified in Paul by life or death
Christ's glory his single aim

schools.summary: Paul's profound summary: 'to me to live is Christ' — for Paul, life itself is summed up in Christ; Christ is the aim, the substance, the whole meaning of his living, to live being to serve, know, and glorify Christ; 'and to die is gain' — death, far from loss, is positive gain for the believer, for it brings him to be with Christ (v.23), freed from sin and sorrow into perfect fellowship with his Lord (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: Christ is the believer's life, so that to live is to have Christ and to die is to gain more of him; the Christian cannot lose, for life is Christ and death is the fuller enjoyment of him.

themes: For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain
Paul's great confession: living is Christ, dying is gain
life summed up in Christ
death a gain bringing fuller fellowship

schools.summary: Paul weighs the alternative: 'if I live in the flesh (continue in this earthly life), this is the fruit of my labour' — continued life means more fruitful labour, more souls won and saints built up, so living has its value; 'yet what I shall choose I wot (know) not' — were the choice his, he cannot tell which to prefer, for both alternatives, life and death, are good, and he is torn between them (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: Paul is caught between his own desire (to depart and be with Christ) and his usefulness to the church (to live and labour); the very difficulty of the choice shows the goodness of both.

themes: if I live in the flesh, this is the fruit of my labour: yet what I shall choose I wot not
Paul's uncertainty whether to prefer life or death
continued life meaning fruitful labour
torn between two goods

schools.summary: Paul is 'in a strait betwixt two (synechomai — hemmed in, pressed, constrained from both sides)' — pulled in two directions by the two good alternatives; 'having a desire to depart (analysai — to loose, as a ship from its moorings, or strike a tent), and to be with Christ' — for himself he longs to die, for death means departing this life to be immediately with Christ; 'which is far better (much more, better by far)' — and this at once upon death, a clear witness that the souls of believers are consciously present with Christ at death, in a state far better than this life (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: to depart and be with Christ is far better; the believer at death passes immediately into the presence of his Lord — the verse a strong proof of the conscious blessedness of the saints with Christ between death and the resurrection.

themes: having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ; which is far better
Paul's desire to depart and be with Christ
the soul consciously with Christ at death
the intermediate state far better

schools.summary: Against his own desire to depart, Paul recognizes another claim: 'to abide (remain) in the flesh (in this earthly life) is more needful for you (the Philippians and the churches)' — his continued life would serve their good; though to depart would be his own gain, to remain would benefit the church, and he is willing to forgo his own preference for their sake, their need outweighing his desire — this consideration tipping the balance toward remaining (v.25, Barnes, Robertson, Clarke).

themes: Nevertheless to abide in the flesh is more needful for you
Paul's remaining more needful for the church
forgoing his own gain for their sake
the church's need outweighing his desire

schools.summary: Paul's persuasion: 'having this confidence, I know that I shall abide (remain alive) and continue with you all' — convinced that the church's need will be met, he expects to be released and to remain for their sake; the end of his remaining being 'for your furtherance (advancement, progress) and joy of faith' — that the Philippians may grow in faith and rejoice in it, his continued ministry serving their spiritual progress and gladness (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): Paul's assurance that he will be spared rests not on certainty of the outcome but on his sense of the church's need and God's purpose to serve it through him.

themes: I know that I shall abide and continue with you all for your furtherance and joy of faith
Paul's confidence that he will remain
for their furtherance and joy of faith
grounded in the church's need

schools.summary: The purpose of his expected release: 'that your rejoicing (kauchema — boasting, glorying) may be more abundant (overflow) in Jesus Christ for me by my coming to you again' — his return would give the Philippians fresh cause for rejoicing; the rejoicing is 'in Jesus Christ,' their gladness at Paul's return being a rejoicing in Christ, for it is Christ who would have spared and restored him, the joy directed to the Lord not merely to Paul (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: That your rejoicing may be more abundant in Jesus Christ for me by my coming to you again
Paul's return increasing their rejoicing in Christ
joy directed to the Lord
the affection between apostle and church

schools.summary: Paul's central exhortation: 'let your conversation (politeuesthe — live as citizens, conduct your public life) be as it becometh (worthily of) the gospel of Christ' — their whole manner of life is to match the gospel they profess; his wish that, whether present or absent, he may hear they 'stand fast (stand firm) in one spirit, with one mind (one soul)' — firm and united, not divided by faction; 'striving together (synathlountes — contending side by side, as athletes or soldiers) for the faith of the gospel' (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Calvin: the believers are to contend side by side for the faith of the gospel, united in the common struggle to defend and advance it against all opposition; unity and steadfastness are the marks of a church worthy of the gospel.

themes: let your conversation be as it becometh the gospel of Christ
that ye stand fast in one spirit, with one mind striving together for the faith of the gospel
the worthy walk standing fast and striving together
united for the gospel

schools.summary: Paul's charge: 'in nothing terrified (ptyromenoi — frightened, alarmed, as startled horses) by your adversaries (opponents, persecutors)' — the believers to stand undaunted, fearless before those who oppose them; their fearless steadfastness 'an evident token (a clear sign, proof)' — 'to them (the adversaries) of perdition (their coming destruction)' and 'to you of salvation' — the believers' courage signifying the foes' ruin and the saints' deliverance; 'and that of God,' the salvation and the courage being from God, his gift and work (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the constancy of believers under persecution is a token to their enemies of their own destruction, and to the faithful of their salvation; their fearlessness is itself a proof that God is with them and will save them.

themes: in nothing terrified by your adversaries
which is to them an evident token of perdition, but to you of salvation, and that of God
fearlessness a token of perdition and salvation
courage the gift of God

schools.summary: 'It is given (echaristhe — granted as a gift, a favour, a privilege)' — both believing and suffering for Christ are graciously bestowed; it is a privilege and an honour to be allowed to believe on Christ and to suffer for him (as Acts 5:41, the apostles rejoicing to be counted worthy to suffer shame for his name). Barnes and Ellicott emphasize the granting of the privilege — the fearlessness of the Christian is a gift of God, not stoic self-sufficiency, and suffering is represented as an honour, not merely a hardship.

Calvin: Paul clearly testifies that faith, as well as constancy in enduring persecutions, is an unmerited gift of God — the knowledge of God is too high for our own attaining, and faith is given for Christ's sake, excluding all merit; this is at variance with the schoolmen, who make later grace a reward of merit for the right use of earlier grace.

Wesley: it is given as a special token of God's love, and of their being in the way of salvation, both to believe and to suffer; Clarke: it is graciously given, an honour God grants only to his most faithful servants, that they should suffer for Christ.

debates.question: Is faith itself the gift of God here, or the granting of the privilege to believe and suffer?

sides.position: Faith itself is an unmerited gift of God, no less than constancy in suffering, excluding all merit
It is the gracious granting of the privilege/honour both to believe and to suffer for Christ

themes: unto you it is given in the behalf of Christ, not only to believe on him, but also to suffer for his sake
to believe and to suffer both granted as a gift
faith as a gift
the honour of suffering for Christ

schools.summary: Paul's conclusion: the Philippians have 'the same conflict (agon — contest, struggle, as in the arena) which ye saw in me, and now hear to be in me' — they share the same struggle of suffering for Christ that they witnessed in Paul; they had 'seen' his conflict when he was beaten and imprisoned at Philippi itself (Acts 16), and 'now hear' of his present conflict in his Roman imprisonment, their own sufferings mirroring his — fellow-sufferers in the same cause, bound together with the apostle (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the believers are not alone in their trials but share the same contest as the apostle and all the saints; the fellowship of suffering binds the church together in the one cause of Christ.

themes: Having the same conflict which ye saw in me, and now hear to be in me
the Philippians sharing the same conflict as Paul
fellow-sufferers in the same cause
the fellowship of Christian suffering
